title: Start here: learn architecture by making one system evolve

lesson.goal: Stop collecting patterns and learn to make justified decisions under constraints.
lesson.outcome: A small architecture decision pack: quality scenarios, one C4 view, one ADR, and evidence from a POC.
lesson.challenge: Use the same running case in every lesson. Do not introduce a new technology until a constraint requires it.

sections.heading: The running case: ParcelFlow
sections.content: You are designing ParcelFlow, a service that lets a customer create a delivery request and follow its progress. In the first release, 2,000 requests/day are expected. A delivery request must never be created twice; sending a notification may be delayed; customer tracking should update within 30 seconds; the team has six engineers and one existing PostgreSQL database. This is intentionally ordinary. Architecture is not reserved for a global-scale system. The same questions appear in a small product: what can fail, who owns a rule, what must be consistent, and how will the team know that it is working?

sections.heading: The learning sequence
sections.content: 1. Quality attributes: turn the case into measurable acceptance criteria. 2. Styles and boundaries: decide what remains in a modular monolith and who owns delivery, billing, and notification data. 3. Integration and data: choose the API and event flow; prove no notification is lost or duplicated. 4. Reliability and operations: define SLOs, traces, alerts, security controls, rollout, and rollback. 5. Documentation: turn the decisions into diagrams, contracts, an ADR, and a runbook. Each sidebar topic is one decision in this sequence — not a list of technologies to memorise.

sections.heading: The question that unlocks each lesson
sections.content: Before reading, answer one question in writing. Quality: what does “fast enough” mean for a customer? Modularity: which team may change a rule without asking another team? Distribution: what does a timeout mean? Data: which data is the source of truth? Operations: how will an on-call engineer discover customer impact? If a lesson does not change your answer to one of those questions, treat it as reference material, not as a decision.

sections.heading: What success looks like
sections.content:
At the end, you should be able to explain: “We start with a modular monolith because the team and release cadence are shared. Order creation writes an outbox record in the same transaction. Billing and notification consume the event idempotently. We accept delayed notification, but not duplicate delivery requests. We measure this through a checkout SLO, consumer lag, traces, and a runbook.”

That sentence is more valuable than being able to recite CQRS, Kubernetes, Zero Trust, or event sourcing without a context.
